output:
**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Does Open-MAGVIT2's asymmetric token factorization, which splits the 18 lookup-free-quantizer bits into two sub-vocabularies by a fixed hand-designed partition chosen to minimize reconstruction error while treating all bits as information-homogeneous and decoupled from the autoregressive model that must predict them, leave reconstruction-optimal tokenizers autoregressively suboptimal because the super-large codebook makes the rFID-to-gFID map non-monotone, so that the bit factorization should instead be co-designed with the AR objective by ordering and grouping bits according to their conditional entropy given spatial context into a coarse-to-fine sub-token hierarchy under an end-to-end rate-distortion-predictability objective?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The deepest contribution here is not another point on an ImageNet accuracy table; it is a change to the DEFAULT objective the field uses to design visual tokenizers. The reigning recipe in tokenizer-based autoregressive (AR) visual generation - inherited from VQGAN and crystallized by MAGVIT-v2's claim that "the tokenizer is key" - trains the quantizer to minimize reconstruction error (rFID) in isolation, then hands the resulting discrete code to a separately trained AR transformer as a fixed prediction target. Open-MAGVIT2 inherits this division of labor exactly: it optimizes a Lookup-Free Quantizer (LFQ) for reconstruction, then splits the 18 LFQ bits by a fixed, hand-designed "asymmetric" partition so a plain AR model can predict two sub-tokens. Predictability-aware tokenization attacks the seam in that pipeline. It says the tokenizer's factorization is not a reconstruction-only object but a PREDICTOR-COUPLED one, and that the correct training signal mixes distortion with the downstream AR model's conditional entropy. That reframing matters beyond any single number because it relocates a quantity the community currently treats as primitive - "lower rFID implies a better generator" - into the space of falsifiable, optimizable design choices.

The structural reason this is important is that the rFID->gFID map is NON-MONOTONE, and the entire field currently selects and scales tokenizers as if it were monotone. A super-large codebook (LFQ's 2^18) is explicitly engineered to drive reconstruction error toward zero, but every bit it adds is also a bit the AR transformer must now predict under spatial context. Reconstruction quality and modeling burden are therefore pulling in OPPOSITE directions: the codebook size that minimizes rFID simultaneously maximizes the AR conditional entropy the generator must absorb. If that is true, then a large and consequential slice of community practice - ranking tokenizers by rFID, scaling codebooks to push rFID down, and comparing tokenizer variants on reconstruction leaderboards - is partly optimizing the wrong objective. This is not a niche concern: it governs how the next generation of tokenizers is built, how the AR-vs-diffusion-tokenizer crossover (MAGVIT-v2, LlamaGen, the diffusion VAE line) is compared, and how labor is divided between "make the tokenizer better" and "make the model bigger."

The practical payoff is concrete and, importantly, budget-neutral, which is what makes the claim adoptable rather than merely interesting. Because the proposal reorders and co-designs bits at FIXED codebook size, FIXED parameter count, and FIXED compute, any gFID improvement it produces is a free lunch in the variables practitioners actually pay for: same tokenizer capacity, same transformer, same FLOPs, better generation. Ordering bits coarse-to-fine by predictability also yields a cheaper, more stable AR factorization - early sub-tokens carry low-conditional-entropy structure that is easy to predict and condition on, which can shorten or simplify the per-position prediction chain and make sampling faster. And it exposes a principled new knob, predictability weight, that controls the tokenizer-vs-model split: turn it up and the tokenizer pre-digests structure for a smaller model; turn it down and you recover the reconstruction-first regime. The field has had no such dial; it has only had "make the codebook bigger," which we argue is the wrong end of the lever. (These improvements are demonstrated on the class-conditional ImageNet benchmark; this is not an endorsement of deployment in synthetic-media or impersonation settings, and ImageNet carries known representation and labeling biases, so generation quality and class coverage may inherit them.)

Finally, a result like this changes future research even if a competitor later builds a better operator, because it ships a reusable, falsifiable REFRAMING alongside the method. Once "better tokenizer -> better generation" is stated as a hypothesis that can FAIL - and demonstrated to fail via an rFID/gFID dissociation, where a tokenizer that is equal or worse on reconstruction yields a better generator - every subsequent tokenizer and AR paper inherits an obligation to report the predictability axis, not just reconstruction. The end-to-end rate-distortion-predictability Lagrangian becomes a co-design objective other works must compare against, the same way rate-distortion became the lingua franca of learned compression. That is the difference between an incremental tokenizer tweak and a contribution that reshapes how the subfield measures, selects, and scales its core component.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The proposal asks for something the source pipeline deliberately avoids: a tokenizer
whose discrete factorization is shaped by how hard the resulting tokens are to predict
auto-regressively, rather than only by how well they reconstruct. That coupling is what
makes the problem hard, and it surfaces five distinct technical obstacles. Each below
states the obstacle, why the obvious approach fails, and the concrete instrument that
makes it tractable.

Obstacle 1 -- The circularity of predictability estimation. The whole premise is that
some groups of LFQ bits carry information that is easy to predict from spatial context
and others carry information that is intrinsically hard; we want to route the easy,
high-mass structure into a coarse sub-token and the residual, near-random detail into
finer sub-tokens. But "easy to predict" is operationally defined as low conditional
entropy under the AR model -- and the AR model is exactly the thing we are training on
top of the tokenizer. The predictability signal we want to optimize the tokenizer
against depends on a model that does not exist yet, and whose own loss depends on the
tokenizer. Naively jointly back-propagating the full AR transformer's cross-entropy into
the quantizer is unstable and self-referential: the tokenizer can lower measured
conditional entropy simply by making the AR model memorize a degenerate token
distribution, and the gradient chases a moving target that is co-adapting to it. The
concrete instrument is a decoupled, low-bias predictability probe rather than the
production AR model. We use a lightweight context model -- a small masked or causal
transformer over the spatial token grid -- maintained as a stop-gradient / EMA proxy:
its parameters track the tokenizer's current code statistics but gradients to the
tokenizer flow only through the entropy estimate, not through the proxy's own weights,
breaking the feedback loop. Equivalently, alternating optimization (freeze tokenizer,
fit proxy to convergence on held-out tokens; freeze proxy, take one tokenizer step)
gives a block-coordinate procedure with a well-defined objective at each half-step. The
proxy is cheap (orders of magnitude smaller than the 343M-1.5B production models), so it
can be re-fit frequently, and its per-bit-group negative log-likelihood is the
predictability statistic the factorization consumes.

Obstacle 2 -- Differentiability of a discrete bit-partition. The factorization is two
discrete decisions: which of the 18 LFQ bits go into which sub-vocabulary (assignment),
and in what coarse-to-fine order the sub-tokens are emitted (ordering). Both are
combinatorial -- a bit-to-group assignment is a discrete map and an ordering is a
permutation -- so the partition that the predictability signal is supposed to optimize
is not differentiable, and there is no gradient to move a bit from one group to another.
The naive fix, "just sort the bits by their marginal variance or marginal entropy and
cut," fails for two reasons: marginal statistics ignore the conditional structure that
actually sets AR difficulty (a bit can be high-variance yet highly predictable from its
neighbors, or low-variance yet conditionally random), and a hard top-k sort admits no
learning signal to correct a bad cut. The concrete instruments are continuous
relaxations with straight-through estimation. For assignment we place a learnable
logit matrix over (bit, sub-vocabulary) and apply Gumbel-softmax / straight-through:
the forward pass uses a hard one-hot routing so the tokenizer stays genuinely discrete,
while the backward pass uses the soft assignment so predictability and reconstruction
gradients can reshape the routing. For ordering we relax the permutation with a
Sinkhorn / soft-permutation operator (Gumbel-Sinkhorn), annealing a doubly-stochastic
matrix toward a hard permutation over training so the coarse-to-fine hierarchy emerges
differentiably and then locks in. This turns "search over partitions" into gradient
descent on a relaxed simplex with a temperature schedule.

Obstacle 3 -- The rate-distortion-predictability tension (you cannot simply minimize
conditional entropy). The seductive but wrong objective is to minimize the AR
conditional entropy of the tokens, because that directly measures "easy to model." But
conditional entropy is trivially minimized by destroying information: a codebook that
collapses many images to the same tokens, or that throws away high-frequency residual,
is extremely predictable and catastrophic for reconstruction. The super-large 2^18 LFQ
codebook exists precisely to push rFID down to ~1.1 at 128x128; an unconstrained
predictability penalty would unwind that and wreck rFID, defeating the point. The
problem is therefore intrinsically a three-way trade-off, not a single minimization. The
concrete instrument is a rate-distortion-predictability Lagrangian that holds
reconstruction (and the codebook rate / utilization that LFQ relies on) as first-class
terms while the predictability term only re-shapes how a fixed information budget is
*partitioned and ordered* across sub-tokens, not how much is kept. Operationally we
constrain rFID to stay within a small tolerance of the Open-MAGVIT2 reconstruction
operating point (~1.1-1.2 @128) -- e.g. a tolerance constraint or a Lagrange weight tuned
so reconstruction is essentially pinned -- and let predictability act in the null space
of reconstruction. The honest framing is that predictability is a redistribution
regularizer over an information budget that distortion and rate define, never a license
to reduce that budget.

Obstacle 4 -- Multi-stage train / inference compounding and exposure bias. The AR stage
predicts sub-token-2 conditioned on sub-token-1 (next-sub-token prediction). At training
the model conditions on ground-truth sub-token-1; at inference it conditions on its own
sampled sub-token-1, so errors in the coarse sub-token propagate and compound into the
fine sub-token, and any predictability gain measured under teacher forcing can evaporate
under free-running sampling. A coarse-to-fine hierarchy makes this sharper, because the
fine sub-tokens are deliberately the conditionally hard residual that depends most on
getting the coarse token right. Naively trusting teacher-forced NLL as the success
metric is exactly the trap. The concrete instruments are scheduled sampling for
sub-tokens (progressively feed the model its own sampled coarse sub-token during
training so the fine head learns to be robust to upstream errors) and an optional
residual correction head that re-predicts or refines the fine sub-token given the
*sampled* coarse context. Critically, all generation metrics (gFID, IS, P/R) are
reported under free-running sampling, and we additionally report the teacher-forced vs
free-running predictability gap as a diagnostic of compounding.

Obstacle 5 -- The mechanism-vs-regularization confound. Any change to the tokenizer that
adds learnable structure, a new loss term, or extra parameters can improve gFID for
reasons that have nothing to do with predictability -- generic regularization, added
capacity, or longer effective training. If we only show "our learned factorization beats
the fixed split," a reviewer cannot tell whether predictability is the cause. The naive
single-comparison design cannot separate the claimed mechanism from these confounds.
The concrete instrument is a mechanism-claim placebo pair plus matched-budget controls.
The structure-destroying placebo keeps every moving part -- the same assignment/ordering
machinery, the same parameter count, the same training compute -- but draws the bit
ordering/grouping at random and freezes it, removing only the predictability signal; if
the gain were generic regularization or capacity, the placebo would still gain, so the
placebo must show ~no improvement over the arbitrary split. We additionally match
params, FLOPs, and AR training steps across all arms, and we instrument the mechanism
directly via per-sub-vocab conditional-entropy curves and representation probes (CKA /
entropy probes) that should change only under the real predictability-driven ordering.
Passing this gate is what licenses the causal claim; failing it is the explicit honest
negative, in which the contribution degrades to a diagnostic of the
reconstruction-generation gap.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The honest answer is that this sits in the unclaimed quadrant between two mature, organizationally separate literatures, and the barrier is a SEAM between communities rather than an unsolved technical lemma in either one. Side A is tokenizer-based AR visual generation: VQ-VAE and VQGAN, MAGVIT-v2's LFQ (Yu et al., "Language Model Beats Diffusion," ICLR 2024), its open replication Open-MAGVIT2, LlamaGen (Sun et al., 2024), VAR (Tian et al., NeurIPS 2024), and TiTok (Yu et al., 2024). This community trains the tokenizer for reconstruction and treats the resulting discrete code as a fixed target; when it factorizes a large vocabulary at all (Open-MAGVIT2's asymmetric bit split), the partition is hand-designed for tractable softmax, never coupled to the predictor's conditional entropy. Side B is neural image compression and information-theoretic source coding, which has owned distortion-rate tradeoffs, entropy coding, and optimal bit allocation for decades. That community knows exactly how to allocate and order bits - but always to minimize STORAGE rate under an entropy coder, never to minimize a downstream AR transformer's predictability burden. Each side has half the toolkit; neither has crossed the predictability axis, because their objectives (perceptual reconstruction vs. compression rate) have given neither a reason to.

It is worth being precise about the closest prior art and where it stops, because the novelty is positional and must be defended against near-neighbors. MAGVIT-v2 and Open-MAGVIT2 LFQ are reconstruction-driven with a fixed split - the very design this work reframes. FSQ (Mentzer et al., ICLR 2024) is lookup-free scalar quantization, conceptually adjacent because it too discards per-code embeddings, but it is still optimized purely for reconstruction and codebook utilization, with no notion of a predictor's conditional entropy. RQ-VAE / RQ-Transformer (Lee et al., CVPR 2022) is the most seductive neighbor: it builds residual DEPTH tokens and is genuinely coarse-to-fine, so a reviewer will ask "isn't this the same?" It is not: RQ's depth ordering follows residual MAGNITUDE for reconstruction fidelity, the stages are not ordered by PREDICTABILITY, and the quantizer is not co-optimized against the AR model's conditional entropy - it is the same reconstruction-first culture wearing a multi-stage coat. VAR (Tian et al., NeurIPS 2024) predicts next-SCALE across spatial resolutions; our axis is WITHIN-token bit predictability, an orthogonal decomposition. MaskGIT (Chang et al., CVPR 2022) orders predictions by confidence, but it does so at INFERENCE during decoding of an already-fixed tokenizer; it is a sampling schedule, not a learned, predictability-ordered tokenizer factorization trained jointly with the AR loss. Learned-compression bit allocation orders bits, but for rate under a storage codec, never for a transformer's predictability.

Beyond the seam itself, there are three concrete barriers that explain the absence even for someone standing in the gap. First is a CIRCULARITY: "predictability" is the conditional entropy of a bit-group given spatial context under the very AR model you are trying to train, so any predictability-ordered factorization appears to require the trained model as input to define the tokenizer that the model is trained on. That chicken-and-egg dependency is exactly the kind of thing that deters a reconstruction-only tokenizer team and a storage-only compression team alike, and it must be broken with an estimator (an alternating or amortized predictability estimate, a proxy conditional-entropy model, or a stop-gradient/EM-style schedule). Second is the RECONSTRUCTION-FIRST training culture of Side A: tokenizers are trained and benchmarked on rFID, papers ship rFID tables, and "is the tokenizer the bottleneck?" has been answered with "make rFID lower," so the community has had neither the objective nor the vocabulary to ask whether a reconstruction-optimal code is predictability-pessimal. Third, and specific to this codebook family, LFQ's lookup-free design makes bit SEMANTICS opaque: with no learned per-code embedding, individual bits carry no obvious interpretable meaning, so the natural question "which bits are easy to predict and should come first?" is non-obvious to even pose, let alone optimize.

What is genuinely new, then, is twofold and both heads must stay visible. The OPERATOR is a predictability-ordered, AR-loss-coupled, end-to-end factorization of the LFQ bits into a coarse-to-fine sub-token hierarchy, trained under a single rate-distortion-predictability Lagrangian (with an optional Hamming-near-perceptual bit geometry so that single-bit prediction errors degrade gracefully). The REFRAMING is the recasting of the reconstruction-generation gap - "the tokenizer is the upper bound; better tokenizer means better generation" - as a falsifiable consequence of DECOUPLED tokenizer and model objectives, predicting a non-monotone rFID->gFID map. Crucially, what separates this positioned novelty from an asserted one is that it is built to be falsified: it collapses by construction to Open-MAGVIT2's exact asymmetric factorization under the boundary B1 (fixed arbitrary partition, identity bit-treatment, predictability weight set to zero); it is guarded by a random-bit-ordering PLACEBO (same operator, same added machinery, but the ordering signal randomized, which must yield no gain, proving the gain comes from predictability and not from extra parameters or a deeper head); and it stands or falls on a measurable rFID/gFID DISSOCIATION, where a factorization matched or worsened on reconstruction nonetheless improves the generator. A neighbor that cannot exhibit that dissociation has not entered this quadrant; one that can has demonstrably crossed the seam.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

The method co-designs the LFQ factorization with the AR objective so that the discrete
token hierarchy is shaped by predictability, not by reconstruction alone. It has five
components, followed by the dataset, metrics, baselines, falsifier ladder, expected
outcomes, and a compute-feasibility assessment.

Component 1 -- The predictability estimator. We attach a lightweight context model -- a
small masked/causal transformer over the spatial grid of tokens, far smaller than the
production AR transformer -- that estimates, for each candidate bit-group, its
conditional entropy given spatial context, i.e. the per-sub-vocabulary negative
log-likelihood in bits per token. To avoid the circularity of Obstacle 1 it is run as a
stop-gradient / EMA proxy (gradients reach the tokenizer only through the entropy
estimate, not the proxy weights) or in an alternating block-coordinate schedule (fit
proxy on held-out tokens, then take a tokenizer step). This estimator is the source of
the predictability signal that drives the factorization.

Component 2 -- The learnable factorization. Two relaxed-discrete modules replace the
fixed hand-designed partition. (a) A differentiable bit-to-sub-vocabulary assignment: a
learnable logit matrix over (bit, sub-vocab) with Gumbel-softmax / straight-through, hard
one-hot in the forward pass (the tokenizer stays genuinely discrete and LFQ-compatible)
and soft in the backward pass. (b) A differentiable ordering into a coarse-to-fine
hierarchy: a Sinkhorn / soft-permutation (Gumbel-Sinkhorn) operator annealed from a
doubly-stochastic matrix to a hard permutation, so easy/high-mass bits flow to the coarse
sub-token and hard residual bits to the fine sub-token(s). Together these turn the
arbitrary 18-bit split into a learned, predictability-ordered partition.

Component 3 -- The end-to-end rate-distortion-predictability objective. The tokenizer is
trained with a Lagrangian L = L_recon + beta * L_rate/commit + lambda_pred * L_pred,
where L_recon is the LFQ reconstruction (pixel + perceptual + adversarial, as in
Open-MAGVIT2) controlling distortion; L_rate/commit is the commitment / entropy /
codebook-utilization term that keeps the 2^18 codebook well-used and controls rate;
and L_pred is the proxy AR cross-entropy / conditional-entropy term that controls how the
fixed information budget is partitioned and ordered across sub-tokens. beta pins
reconstruction near the Open-MAGVIT2 operating point (we additionally impose an rFID
tolerance so predictability acts only in the null space of reconstruction, per Obstacle
3); lambda_pred controls the strength of the predictability redistribution and is swept
to trace the rate-distortion-predictability frontier. Setting lambda_pred = 0 with a
fixed partition and identity bit map recovers Open-MAGVIT2 exactly (the B1 collapse).

Component 4 -- Optional Hamming ~ perceptual bit geometry. Because LFQ codes are bit
vectors and decoding is lookup-free, we can optionally regularize the code geometry so
that Hamming-adjacent codes are perceptually adjacent (small Hamming distance ->
small perceptual/reconstruction difference). This makes single-bit AR prediction errors
perceptually graceful and aligns the discrete metric the AR model operates in with the
continuous metric reconstruction cares about. It is ablated separately so its effect is
not confounded with the predictability ordering.

Component 5 -- The AR generation stage. Over the learned coarse-to-fine hierarchy we run
plain AR with next-sub-token prediction: predict the coarse sub-token, then the fine
sub-token(s) conditioned on it, spatially in raster order, reusing the Open-MAGVIT2
transformer backbones (B/L/XL). To address compounding / exposure bias (Obstacle 4) we
add scheduled sampling for sub-tokens (progressively condition the fine head on the
model's own sampled coarse sub-token) and an optional residual correction head that
refines the fine sub-token given sampled coarse context. All generation numbers are
reported under free-running sampling.

DATASET. ImageNet at 128x128 and 256x256 -- the Open-MAGVIT2 benchmark -- reusing the
open Open-MAGVIT2 codebase for exact reproduction of the tokenizer and AR stages.

METRICS. Reconstruction: rFID (target ~1.1-1.2 @128, matching the source operating
point). Generation: gFID (primary), Inception Score, and Precision/Recall (to catch
mode-coverage vs fidelity trade-offs). Predictability: per-sub-vocabulary NLL /
conditional entropy in bits per token, reported teacher-forced and free-running. The
headline diagnostic is the rFID-vs-gFID curve across factorizations, which exposes the
non-monotonicity at the heart of the spine. We note that rFID, gFID, IS, and
Precision/Recall measure distributional properties of a benchmark split and do not
constitute deployment-readiness or safety guarantees; a public release of such a
generator should include provenance metadata and consider watermarking under
responsible-release norms.

BASELINES. Open-MAGVIT2-B/L/XL as the exact-reproduction B1 anchor (their reported
rFID/gFID/IS), plus LlamaGen, MaskGIT / MAGVIT-v2 reported numbers, and VAR for external
context. Our arms are matched to these on params, FLOPs, and AR training steps.

EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN / FALSIFIER LADDER.
1. B1 exact-collapse unit test: lambda_pred = 0, fixed hand-designed partition, identity
   bit map -- must reproduce Open-MAGVIT2-B/L/XL reported rFID and gFID to within
   reproduction noise. This is a harness gate; if it fails the implementation is wrong,
   not the hypothesis.
2. Random-order PLACEBO: the full factorization machinery with the bit ordering/grouping
   drawn at random and frozen (same params, same compute, predictability signal removed).
   Expected: no gFID gain over the arbitrary split. If the placebo gains, the effect is
   generic regularization/capacity, not predictability.
3. rFID/gFID DISSOCIATION (central claim): the learned tokenizer may have equal or
   slightly worse rFID than Open-MAGVIT2 yet better gFID at matched params/compute,
   demonstrating that minimizing reconstruction is not the same as minimizing modeling
   burden. Expected direction: rFID within ~+0.0 to +0.1 of the anchor, gFID improved by
   a meaningful margin (plausibly ~0.3-1.0 gFID at B/L scale; to be measured), with the
   rFID-vs-gFID curve visibly non-monotone.
4. Synthetic-injection CALIBRATION anchor: build a controlled token-generating process
   with a planted per-bit conditional-entropy ordering; the proxy estimator must recover
   that ordering (rank correlation bounded away from zero) before we trust its ImageNet
      estimates. This calibrates Component 1 independent of the production model. A second
      null control completes the mechanism-claim placebo pair: in an absent-mechanism regime
      where the bits are constructed to be information-homogeneous (the proxy then reports
      near-uniform per-bit conditional entropy, leaving no predictability structure to
      exploit), the learned factorization must yield no gFID gain over the arbitrary split,
      exactly as the random-order placebo does; a gain in either null condition (random
      ordering, or the information-homogeneous regime) falsifies the predictability
      attribution.
5. Ablations: remove each objective term (L_recon, L_rate/commit, L_pred) one at a time;
   ordering-only vs assignment-only; EMA-proxy vs alternating vs context-probe estimator;
   Hamming-geometry on/off; scheduled-sampling / residual-head on/off. Each isolates one
   causal pathway.
6. Mechanism instrumentation: per-sub-vocab conditional-entropy curves and CKA / entropy
   probes should move only under the real predictability ordering, not the placebo.
7. Scaling check: B (343M) -> L (804M) -> XL (1.5B); the gain must persist or grow with
   scale, otherwise it is a small-model artifact.

EXPECTED OUTCOMES / HONEST NEGATIVE. If predictability-aware factorization is real, we
expect: B1 reproduces the source numbers; the placebo shows ~no gain; the learned
tokenizer dissociates rFID from gFID (equal-or-slightly-worse rFID, better gFID at
matched budget); the mechanism probes move only for the real ordering; and the gain holds
across B/L/XL. The honest negative is explicit and pre-registered: if there is no gFID
improvement at matched rFID/params/compute, OR the random-order placebo matches the
learned factorization, OR the absent-mechanism (information-homogeneous) regime shows
a gain over the arbitrary split, then the predictability hypothesis is not supported, and the
contribution degrades to a diagnostic that characterizes and quantifies the
reconstruction-generation (rFID->gFID) gap and the non-monotonicity of the
rFID-vs-gFID map -- still a useful negative result about why lower rFID need not mean
lower gFID.

COMPUTE FEASIBILITY (honest). Full ImageNet-scale AR training at XL (1.5B) is non-trivial,
and the falsifier ladder multiplies arms. We mitigate as follows: (a) reuse the public
Open-MAGVIT2 pretrained checkpoints as the B1 anchor and as warm starts, so we are not
training every baseline from scratch; (b) develop and ablate the tokenizer-factorization
at smaller scale and at 128x128 first, where the tokenizer and a small AR head are cheap,
and only promote the winning configuration to L/XL and 256x256 for the scaling check;
(c) the added machinery is cheap relative to AR training -- the proxy context model is
orders of magnitude smaller than the production transformer, and the Sinkhorn /
linear-assignment relaxations over 18 bits are negligible cost. The dominant cost remains
the AR transformer training that the baselines already pay, so the incremental cost of
testing predictability is small relative to the experiment as a whole.
